learning_objectives: Identify applications of behaviorist principles in educational settings
Critique the limitations of behaviorist approaches to learning
readings: Learning Theories: An Educational Perspective Ch. 2
Skinner, B.F. - The Technology of Teaching (excerpts)

lecture_notes.introduction: Behaviorism was the dominant paradigm in psychology and education for much of the 20th century. Understanding its principles is essential for evaluating modern educational technology, much of which still relies on behaviorist mechanisms.
key_concepts: Limitations: ignores cognition, motivation, and social context
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of a modern educational app (Duolingo) through a behaviorist lens: identifying reinforcement schedules, feedback loops, and gamification elements
setup_requirements: Access to Duolingo app for demonstration

review.value: Behavior is shaped by consequences: positive reinforcement increases frequency, punishment decreases it, but punishment has significant side effects in educational settings
Skinner's teaching machines broke content into small frames with immediate feedback — a direct ancestor of computer-based training

learning_objectives: Describe the information processing model of memory
Explain schema theory and its implications for instruction
Apply cognitive load theory to instructional design decisions
readings: Learning Theories: An Educational Perspective Ch. 5

lecture_notes.introduction: The cognitive revolution shifted focus from observable behavior to internal mental processes. Cognitivism treats the mind as an information processor and has profound implications for how we design instruction.
key_concepts: Sensory memory, working memory, long-term memory
Schema theory and prior knowledge activation
Cognitive load theory: intrinsic, extraneous, germane load
Dual coding theory: verbal and visual channels
lecture_notes.demonstration: Memory experiment: demonstrate the limits of working memory using digit span tasks and chunking exercises
setup_requirements: Multimedia presentation slides for dual coding demonstration

review.value: Miller's 7±2 items; Cowan's revised estimate of 4 chunks; implications for how much new information to present at once
Instruction should minimize extraneous load (poor design), manage intrinsic load (complexity), and optimize germane load (schema building)

learning_objectives: Compare Piaget's cognitive constructivism with Vygotsky's social constructivism
Explain the zone of proximal development and scaffolding
Design learning activities that apply constructivist principles
readings: Learning Theories: An Educational Perspective Ch. 6
Vygotsky, L.S. - Mind in Society, Ch. 6

lecture_notes.introduction: Constructivism argues that learners actively build knowledge through interaction with their environment and with others. This session examines two foundational constructivist theories and their implications for teaching.
key_concepts: Scaffolding and gradual release of responsibility
Social constructivism: knowledge as socially negotiated
lecture_notes.demonstration: ZPD mapping activity: identify a skill, define what a learner can do alone, with help, and not yet — then design scaffolding for the middle zone
setup_requirements: Example teaching scenarios for scaffolding design

review.value: Piaget emphasized individual cognitive development through stages; Vygotsky emphasized the social and cultural nature of learning through mediated activity
The gap between what a learner can do independently and what they can do with guidance — the optimal space for instruction

class_schedule.topic: Connectivism and Learning in the Digital Age
learning_objectives: Explain connectivism as a learning theory for the networked era
Analyze how digital networks change the nature of knowledge and learning
Evaluate the strengths and criticisms of connectivism
readings: Siemens, G. - Connectivism: A Learning Theory for the Digital Age
Downes, S. - An Introduction to Connective Knowledge

lecture_notes.introduction: Connectivism proposes that learning occurs through building and traversing networks of information. In a world where knowledge is distributed across digital networks, knowing where to find information may be as important as knowing the information itself.
key_concepts: Learning as network formation and navigation
Nodes, connections, and the role of weak ties
Critique: is connectivism a theory or a pedagogical approach?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map your personal learning network: identify the people, platforms, feeds, and communities where you learn informally
setup_requirements: Network mapping tools or large paper for drawing
Access to personal social media and learning accounts for reflection

review.value: Learning is the process of creating connections between nodes of information; the capacity to know more is more critical than what is currently known
Some scholars argue connectivism describes a phenomenon (networked learning) rather than explaining the cognitive mechanisms by which learning occurs

learning_objectives: Distinguish between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation
Apply self-determination theory to educational design
Describe strategies for fostering self-regulated learning
readings: Learning Theories: An Educational Perspective Ch. 9
Deci, E. & Ryan, R. - Self-Determination Theory overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Motivation is the engine of learning. Without understanding what drives learners, even the best-designed instruction will fail. This session examines major motivation theories and their practical implications.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesign a demotivating assignment: take a compliance-driven task and restructure it to support autonomy, competence, and relatedness

review.value: People are most motivated when three basic psychological needs are met: autonomy (choice), competence (mastery), and relatedness (belonging)
Extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, particularly when rewards are expected, tangible, and contingent on task completion

learning_objectives: Describe cognitive and social-emotional development from childhood through adulthood
Explain how developmental stage affects learning capacity and preference
Apply developmental theory to the design of age-appropriate instruction
readings: Learning Theories: An Educational Perspective Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective education is developmentally appropriate. A lesson designed for a 7-year-old must differ fundamentally from one designed for a 17-year-old or a 47-year-old. This session surveys human development with an eye toward instructional implications.
key_concepts: Piaget's stages: sensorimotor through formal operational
Erikson's psychosocial stages and their educational relevance
Adolescent brain development and executive function
Implications for educational technology design across ages
lecture_notes.demonstration: Age-appropriate redesign challenge: adapt a single learning objective for three age groups (8, 16, 40) considering developmental characteristics
setup_requirements: Sample learning objectives for adaptation exercise

review.value: Knowles argued adults are self-directed, draw on experience, need to know why they are learning, and are problem-oriented rather than subject-oriented
The prefrontal cortex (planning, impulse control) continues developing into the mid-20s, which affects risk-taking, motivation, and self-regulation

questions.question: A student uses flashcards with spaced repetition to study vocabulary. Which learning theory best explains why this technique is effective, and what specific cognitive mechanism is at work?
questions.answer: Cognitivism best explains spaced repetition. The technique leverages the spacing effect: distributing practice over time strengthens memory traces during encoding and retrieval. Each retrieval attempt strengthens the neural pathway, moving information from working memory to long-term memory. The desirable difficulty of recalling at increasing intervals promotes deeper encoding compared to massed practice.

rubric.key: Explains spacing effect and retrieval practice

questions.question: Compare how a behaviorist and a constructivist would explain a student learning to solve quadratic equations. What would instruction look like under each approach?
questions.answer: A behaviorist would view learning as acquiring a set of procedural steps through practice and reinforcement. Instruction would involve demonstrating the formula, providing drills with immediate feedback, and rewarding correct answers. A constructivist would view learning as building understanding of why the formula works and when to apply it. Instruction would involve exploring patterns, manipulating visual models, discussing with peers, and discovering the relationship between coefficients and solutions.

rubric.key: Clear contrast between the two approaches

questions.question: According to self-determination theory, what three basic psychological needs must be met for intrinsic motivation? Give a specific example of how an online course could satisfy each need.
questions.answer: Autonomy: allow students to choose which topics to explore in depth or select from multiple assignment formats. Competence: provide progressive challenges with clear feedback so students experience mastery without overwhelming frustration. Relatedness: incorporate discussion forums, peer review, or collaborative projects so learners feel connected to a learning community.

rubric.key: Provides specific, feasible online course example for each
Examples demonstrate understanding of the underlying theory

questions.question: How does Knowles' theory of andragogy differ from traditional pedagogy? Identify two implications for designing educational technology for adult learners.
questions.answer: Andragogy assumes adults are self-directed, bring rich prior experience, are motivated by relevance to real-life problems, and need to understand why they are learning something. Traditional pedagogy assumes teacher-directed instruction with subject-centered organization. Implications for ed tech: (1) Provide learner control over pacing, sequencing, and depth rather than linear, locked pathways. (2) Use authentic, problem-based scenarios drawn from real workplace situations rather than abstract academic exercises.

rubric.key: Accurate description of andragogy vs. pedagogy
First implication is specific and well-reasoned
Second implication is specific and well-reasoned

major_assignments.title: Learning Theory Application Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select an existing educational technology product (e.g., Khan Academy, Coursera, Minecraft Education, Duolingo). Analyze it through the lens of at least three learning theories covered in this course. Identify which theoretical principles the product embodies, where it aligns with research on learning, and where it falls short. Conclude with evidence-based recommendations for improvement.
objectives: Apply multiple learning theories to analyze a real educational product
Evaluate alignment between design decisions and learning science
Demonstrate scholarly writing with integration of primary sources
deliverables: Annotated screenshots illustrating key design features analyzed

criteria: Accurately applies at least three learning theories
Identifies specific product features and maps them to theoretical principles
Demonstrates deep understanding of each theory applied
Identifies both strengths and weaknesses of the product
Considers multiple perspectives and potential biases
Recommendations are grounded in learning theory
Sophisticated analysis connecting learning theory to product design
Critical evaluation grounded in empirical evidence

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on learning theory concepts and applications

participation.description: In-class activities, demonstrations, and discussion contributions

midterm_examination.description: Covers behaviorism, cognitivism, constructivism, and connectivism

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam emphasizing application of theories to educational design scenarios

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade by one half-letter. Active participation requires presence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 5% per day for up to 5 days. Extensions must be requested at least 48 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Study groups are encouraged. All written work must be individually authored. Cite all sources using APA 7th edition.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism or submitting another's work results in a zero for the assignment and referral to the Office of Academic Integrity.
course_policies.technology_requirements: A computer with internet access for research and LMS access. Access to at least one educational technology product for analysis assignments.
